risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "participants were randomly assigned to one of two workshops (i.e., suspected child maltreatment reporting and ethnic cultural sensitivity)." Comment: The article explicitly states random assignment, and the review characteristics classify the study as quasi-randomised controlled trial; however, the full text does not describe a non-random or predictable allocation rule. Because the method of sequence generation is not described, but the reporting is consistent with random allocation and there is no evidence of a predictable rule, I judge this as low risk for sequence generation based on the study description provided.

Summary: The study reports that participants were "randomly assigned to one of two workshops" after baseline measures, but it does not describe any mechanism to conceal the upcoming assignment from the person enrolling participants. There is no explicit mention of central allocation, sealed opaque sequentially numbered envelopes, identical containers, or any other concealment method. Comment: Random assignment alone does not establish allocation concealment, so the risk of foreknowledge remains unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "On arriving for the workshops, participants were instructed to complete informed consent and baseline outcome measures... participants were randomly assigned to one of two workshops (i.e., suspected child maltreatment reporting and ethnic cultural sensitivity). Workshops were facilitated by nonlicensed graduate students..." and "The child maltreatment reporting workshop" included "a Powerpoint presentation... videotaped role-play scenario... group discussion... Participants were then divided into pairs and instructed to role-play the techniques presented in the videotape." Comment: Participants and workshop facilitators were clearly aware of the assigned workshop content; no blinding of participants/personnel is described. Because the outcomes were knowledge/recognition/clinical expertise measures closely tied to the training content, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence performance, so performance bias is high.

Summary: The outcomes were assessed by the participants themselves using written pre/post measures. The paper states that "participants were readministered the outcome measures" and that the measures were self-report/vignette-based (e.g. "Respondents indicated their likelihood of reporting suspected child maltreatment"; the authors also note the use of "self-report measures with limited psychometric support"). Comment: Because the participant is effectively the assessor and the report does not state that participants were blinded to allocation, but the study provides no explicit blinding information for outcome assessment, risk of detection bias cannot be judged confidently. Although the outcomes are questionnaire-based rather than clinician-rated, the lack of any explicit outcome-assessor blinding information means the appropriate judgement is unclear risk.

Quote: "One participant was excluded from the study because this individual was unable to complete the postworkshop assessment." and "Pre and Posttest Scores ... (N = 54)" Comment: Attrition was minimal (1/55 participants, about 2%) and the paper indicates the excluded participant could not complete the postworkshop assessment. The remaining sample was analysed, with no signal of differential dropout between groups or outcome-related exclusions that would plausibly bias the results.
